content:
3 min read
Just now

--

Press enter or click to view image in full size

For years, the goal of test automation was straightforward: automate more tests, execute them faster, and reduce manual effort.
That mindset worked when software changed at a predictable pace.
Today, it doesn’t.
Modern engineering teams deploy multiple times a day, architectures are becoming increasingly distributed, and AI is changing how software is built. The challenge is no longer how many tests we can automate.
The challenge is making better engineering decisions.
This is where Artificial Intelligence is transforming Quality Engineering.

The biggest mistake I see
Most teams start using AI by asking it to generate test cases.
There’s nothing wrong with that.
But in my experience, that’s one of the least valuable applications of AI in QA.
Generating test cases saves a few minutes.
Helping engineers make better decisions can save days — or even prevent production incidents.
The real value of AI is not replacing engineers.
It’s augmenting engineering judgment.

1. Use AI to review User Stories
Before development even starts, I ask AI to analyze User Stories.
A simple prompt like:
“Review this User Story and identify missing acceptance criteria, edge cases, and possible test scenarios.”
Almost every time, AI finds assumptions the team hadn’t discussed.
Examples include:

• Missing validation rules

• Error handling scenarios

• Permission-related edge cases

• Boundary value conditions

• Negative test cases
Instead of writing tests later, you improve the requirements first.
That’s Shift Left in practice.

2. Analyze Pull Requests before running tests
One of my favorite workflows is asking AI to review Pull Requests from a testing perspective.
Instead of asking:
“Review this code.”
I ask something like:
“Based on this Pull Request, which parts of the application are most likely to break? What regression tests should I prioritize?”
The response usually highlights:

• Impacted modules

• Hidden dependencies

• Possible regressions

• Risk level

• Suggested exploratory testing
This helps prioritize testing instead of executing every possible test blindly.

3. Investigate production failures
Production bugs often generate hundreds of log lines.
Finding the root cause can take hours.
AI can significantly speed up this process.
I usually provide:

• Error logs

• Stack traces

• Screenshots

• User actions

• Recent code changes
Then I ask:
“What are the most likely root causes? Which automated test could have prevented this issue?”
Even when the first answer isn’t perfect, AI quickly narrows the investigation.
Instead of starting from zero, engineers start with several strong hypotheses.

4. Review your automation strategy
Another underrated use case is reviewing existing automated tests.
Instead of asking AI to create new tests, ask it to evaluate the ones you already have.
For example:

• Which critical user journeys are not covered?

• Are these tests maintainable?

• Which tests are likely to become flaky?

• Which scenarios are duplicated?

• What should be automated first?
This often reveals gaps that aren’t obvious during day-to-day development.

AI should support decisions — not replace them
One concern I frequently hear is:
“Will AI replace QA Engineers?”
I don’t think so.
Quality has never been about executing tests.
Quality is about understanding risk.
It’s about asking the right questions.
It’s about knowing what deserves attention.
AI doesn’t replace that expertise.
It amplifies it.
The engineers who will benefit the most from AI aren’t necessarily the ones writing the best prompts.
They’re the ones who understand software quality deeply enough to ask meaningful questions.

The future of Quality Engineering
I believe the future of QA isn’t about generating more automated tests.
It’s about building systems that continuously help engineers answer questions like:

• What should I test first?

• Where is the highest risk?

• Which change deserves more attention?

• What am I missing?
Those are engineering decisions.
And AI is becoming an excellent assistant for making them.
The future of Quality Engineering won’t be defined by how many tests we automate.
It will be defined by how intelligently we use AI to improve software quality.
How are you using AI in your Quality Engineering workflow?
Are you already using it beyond code generation, or is that still your primary use case?
